edges.description: Persistent vascular inflammation contributes to later remodeling and structural complications such as aneurysm, dissection, or ischemia.
edges.source: Immune-mediated aortic wall inflammation in large-vessel vasculitis
edges.target: Aortic wall remodeling and structural complications

edges.description: Infectious wall injury commonly presents with aneurysmal aortic disease.
edges.source: Infectious aortic wall infection and aneurysmal injury

edges.description: Fibrotic inflammatory large-vessel disease can contribute to aneurysm, dissection, or rupture.
edges.target: Aortic wall remodeling and structural complications

edges.description: Aortic wall remodeling can present as aneurysmal dilation.
edges.source: Aortic wall remodeling and structural complications

edges.description: Weakened and inflamed aortic wall can dissect.
edges.source: Aortic wall remodeling and structural complications

nodes.description: Aortic wall inflammation can weaken the vessel wall and contribute to aneurysmal dilation.

evidence.reference_title: Aortitis Increases the Risk of Surgical Complications and Re-Operations After Major Aortic Surgery
evidence.snippet: Aortitis, defined as inflammation of the aorta, can lead to aneurysms and dissections.

evidence.reference_title: Retrospective Multicenter Study Comparing Infectious and Noninfectious Aortitis
evidence.snippet: IA was more frequently associated with aortic aneurysms compared with NIA (78.8% vs 17.6%, P < .001), especially located in the abdominal aorta (69.7% vs 23.1%, P < .001).

nodes.description: Severe inflammatory weakening of the aorta can predispose to dissection.

evidence.reference_title: Aortitis Increases the Risk of Surgical Complications and Re-Operations After Major Aortic Surgery
evidence.snippet: Aortitis, defined as inflammation of the aorta, can lead to aneurysms and dissections.

nodes.description: Inflammatory injury can weaken and remodel the aortic wall, producing aneurysm or dissection and driving the need for long-term aortic follow-up.
nodes.id: Aortic wall remodeling and structural complications

evidence.reference_title: Aortitis Increases the Risk of Surgical Complications and Re-Operations After Major Aortic Surgery
evidence.snippet: Aortitis, defined as inflammation of the aorta, can lead to aneurysms and dissections.

nodes.description: IgG4-related aortitis/periaortitis involves chronic fibrotic inflammation with IgG4-bearing plasma-cell infiltration in large-vessel tissue.

evidence.reference_title: Unraveling the complexity of IgG4-related aortitis and periarteritis: from pathogenesis to clinical practice
evidence.snippet: IgG4-related disease (IgG4-RD) is a chronic fibrotic inflammatory condition characterized by elevated serum IgG4 levels and the infiltration of IgG4-bearing plasma cells in affected organs.

nodes.description: Large-vessel vasculitis-associated aortitis involves immune-mediated inflammation of the vascular wall, including inflammatory cell accumulation in aortic tissue in Takayasu arteritis.
nodes.id: Immune-mediated aortic wall inflammation in large-vessel vasculitis

evidence.reference_title: Imaging in Large Vessel Vasculitis-A Narrative Review
evidence.snippet: Both conditions involve immune-mediated inflammation of the vascular wall, despite differing in epidemiology and presentation.

evidence.reference_title: Accumulation of Lymphocytes, Dendritic Cells, and Granulocytes in the Aortic Wall Affected by Takayasu's Disease
evidence.snippet: CD68 (macrophages), CD15 (granulocytes), von Willebrand factor (endothelial cells), and alpha-smooth muscle actin (smooth muscle cells).

evidence.snippet: (endothelial cells), and alpha-smooth muscle actin (smooth muscle cells).

nodes.description: Infectious aortitis is initiated by microbial infection of native aortic tissue, producing wall thickening or aneurysmal distortion and a higher mortality-risk phenotype than noninfectious aortitis.
nodes.id: Infectious aortic wall infection and aneurysmal injury

evidence.reference_title: Retrospective Multicenter Study Comparing Infectious and Noninfectious Aortitis
evidence.snippet: One hundred eighty-three patients were included. Of these, 66 had IA (36.1%); the causative organism was Enterobacterales and streptococci in 18.2% each

evidence.reference_title: Retrospective Multicenter Study Comparing Infectious and Noninfectious Aortitis
